patient_profile.chief_complaint: Valid confusion and profound weakness found by son.
patient_profile.hpi: Ms. Williams has had a UTI for 1 week but didn't take her antibiotics. She has been drinking juice to 'flush it out'. Her son found her lethargic and unable to stand. He states she has been urinating frequently for days.

ros.gen: Severe dehydration, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor.
ros.resp: No Kussmaul respirations. Breath does NOT smell fruity.
ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Retired veteran. Lives in assisted living. Former smoker (quit 20 years ago).

exhibits.title: Electrolytes
exhibits.content: Sodium: 148 mEq/L (Hypernatremia due to dehydration). Potassium: 4.8 mEq/L. BUN: 60 mg/dL (Pre-renal azotemia).

steps.prompt: The nurse reviews the lab results. Differentiate HHS from DKA based on Ms. Williams' presentation.
steps.rationale: HHS (Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State) is characterized by extreme hyperglycemia (>600) and hyperosmolarity (>320) leading to profound dehydration and neurological changes. Unlike DKA, there is enough circulating insulin to prevent ketosis/acidosis.

steps.prompt: Which orders does the nurse anticipate for initial resuscitation? Select all that apply.
options: Administer IV Regular Insulin Bolus (0.1 units/kg)
steps.rationale: Fluid resuscitation is the absolute priority to fix the hyperosmolarity. IV Insulin is used to lower BG. Potassium must be monitored as insulin shifts K+ into cells. A Foley is needed to track output accurately during massive fluid replacement.

steps.prompt: During treatment, the patient becomes responsive but complains of muscle cramps. Her K+ drops to 3.2 mEq/L. Prioritize the plan.

options: Add Potassium Chloride to IV Fluids
answer: Add Potassium Chloride to IV Fluids

steps.rationale: Insulin drives Potassium into the cell. If K+ drops below 3.3, insulin MUST be held to prevent life-threatening arrhythmias (VT/VF). Repletion (IV KCl) is urgent. EKG provides real-time monitoring of cardiac stability.

patient_profile.hpi: Ben just finished soccer practice. He took his insulin before practice but didn't eat his snack. He is alert but anxious.

ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Retired school teacher. Lives with spouse. Nonsmoker. Occasional alcohol.

steps.prompt: Classify the patient's symptoms as Adrenergic (catecholamine release) or Neuroglycopenic (brain glucose deprivation).
steps.rationale: The body releases epinephrine to raise BG, causing shaking/sweating/tachycardia. If uncorrected, the brain runs out of fuel, causing confusion/coma (Neuroglycopenia).

steps.prompt: Ben is conscious and can swallow. Which food items serve as appropriate '15g' rapid-acting carbohydrates? Select all that apply.
options: Standard size Bagel with Cream Cheese
steps.rationale: Treatment requires simple sugars. Diet soda has no sugar. Chocolate and Bagels contain fat/protein which delays absorption.

steps.prompt: Ben's roommate finds him later unconscious and unable to swallow. BG is 30 mg/dL. Prioritize the emergency response.
steps.rationale: An unconscious patient is an aspiration risk; NEVER put anything in their mouth. IM Glucagon mobilizes liver glycogen. 911 is needed. Monitoring airway (side-lying position) is critical due to vomiting risk from Glucagon.

patient_profile.hpi: Lisa notes her BG has been >300 mg/dL for 4 hours despite bolusing via pump. Pump shows 'No Delivery'. She feels nauseous.

ros.skin: Pump site is red and tender (possible kink/infection).
ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.

patient_profile.sh: Construction worker. Lives alone. Smokes 1 pack/day. Social drinker.

exhibits.title: Ketone Strip
exhibits.content: Result: Moderate Ketones Detected. (Risk of DKA)

steps.prompt: Troubleshoot the pump failure. Match the potential cause to the component.
steps.rationale: Systematic troubleshooting: Check the Site (kinks, infection), the Line (air bubbles, clogs), and the Pump (battery, settings).

steps.prompt: The pump is confirmed to be malfunctioning. What are the priority actions to prevent DKA? Select all that apply.
options: Administer a correction bolus via syringe/pen (Manual injection)
Keep bolusing with the pump until alarm clears
Calculate total daily dose for backup plan
Change the infusion set and new site rotation
steps.rationale: Manual insulin is required to fix the high BG immediately because the pump is unreliable. Disconnecting prevents accidental bolus if it suddenly works. Ketones confirm insulin deficiency.

steps.prompt: Ketones are positive. The patient needs a backup plan while the pump is off. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Only Short-acting insulin is in the pump. When disconnected, the patient has NO basal insulin. They need manual Long-Acting (Basal) and Rapid-Acting (Bolus) to survive until the pump is replaced. Monitoring for ketone clearance is vital.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: Waking up with high blood sugar every morning.
patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Oldman reports his fasting BG is ~250 mg/dL every morning for the past week. He thought his insulin wasn't working, so he INCREASED his bedtime NPH dose 3 days ago, but the morning numbers got WORSE. He reports vivid nightmares and night sweats.
patient_profile.pmh: Type 2 Diabetes (Insulin dependent), Obstructive Sleep Apnea.

ros.gen: Waking up with damp sheets (Night sweats).
ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Software engineer. Works from home. High stress. Enjoys hiking.

exhibits.title: Glucose Log
exhibits.content:
Bedtime (2200): 110 mg/dL
3 AM: 55 mg/dL (Hypoglycemia)
Breakfast (0700): 260 mg/dL (Rebound Hyperglycemia)

steps.prompt: Interpret the cause of morning hyperglycemia based on the 3AM check.
rows: Caused by natural Growth Hormone release
steps.rationale: Somogyi = Rebound hyperglycemia following nocturnal hypoglycemia (Low at 3AM). The body panic-dumps sugar. Dawn = Natural circadian rhythm GH release causing insulin resistance (High/Normal at 3AM). Night sweats are a sign of hypoglycemia.

steps.prompt: Based on the diagnosis of Somogyi Effect, which interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Check BG at 3 AM for the next few nights
Switch dinner to an earlier time
steps.rationale: The problem is Hypoglycemia while sleeping. We need to prevent the drop. Decreasing the insulin or adding a snack helps carry the sugar through the night. Increasing the dose (which the patient did) worsens the effect.

steps.prompt: A month later, Gary returns. His 3AM BG is now 140, but his morning fasting is 180. Prioritize the new plan.
steps.rationale: If the 3AM blood sugar is normal/high, and morning is high, it is Dawn Phenomenon (not enough insulin to cover the GH surge). The treatment is to INCREASE the insulin to cover the dawn rise.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: Flu symptoms and loss of appetite.
patient_profile.hpi: Tina has the flu. She hasn't eaten in 24 hours due to nausea. She called the clinic asking if she should skip her Lantus (Basal) insulin tonight because she 'didn't eat dinner'.

ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.

patient_profile.sh: Nursing home resident. Widowed. Assistance required for ADLs.

steps.prompt: The nurse educates Tina on 'Sick Day Rules'. Which instructions are vital? Select all that apply.
options: NEVER stop taking your insulin, even if not eating
Check urine ketones if BG > 240 mg/dL
Force fluids (4-6 oz every 30 mins) to prevent dehydration
If you cannot eat solid food, drink sugar-containing liquids (juice/gatorade) to maintain BG
Go to the ER immediately for temperature > 100 F
steps.rationale: Illness increases cortisol, which raises BG even without food. Hydration is key to flushing ketones. Carbohydrates are still needed to prevent starvation ketosis, so liquid calories are used if solids aren't tolerated. Fever alone isn't an ER trip unless persistent/high.

steps.prompt: Tina asks when she needs to call the doctor or go to the ER. Classify the scenarios.
rows: Vomiting more than once / unable to verify fluids
Blood Glucose > 250 for > 24 hours despite extra insulin
Moderate to Large Ketones in urine
columns: Continue Sick Day Management at Home

answer.Temperature of 100.5 F: Continue Sick Day Management at Home

steps.rationale: Inability to keep fluids down is an emergency (DKA risk). Moderate/Large ketones indicate impending DKA. Mild fever is expected with flu.

steps.prompt: Tina calls back 6 hours later. She can't stop vomiting and feels 'breathless'. Prioritize the complication.

options: Advise to go to ER immediately
answer: Advise to go to ER immediately

steps.rationale: Persistent vomiting + Breathlessness (Kussmaul) + T1DM = DKA until proven otherwise. Home management has failed. She needs IV fluids and insulin. We monitor the Anion Gap to gauge acidosis severity.

patient_profile.hpi: Maria is G3 P2 at 26 weeks gestation. Her 1-hour GTT came back at 155 mg/dL. She admits to craving pasta and ice cream.
patient_profile.pmh: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS), Obesity (BMI 31). Prev baby was 9lbs 2oz.

ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.neuro: Sensation intact in extremities (or diminished if neuropathy).
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Office manager. Married with 2 children. Nonsmoker. Denies alcohol use.

steps.prompt: The patient is diagnosed with GDM. Assess the risks to the Fetus vs the Mother.
steps.rationale: High sugar crosses the placenta (fat baby), but insulin does NOT. The baby makes its own insulin, leading to hypoglycemia at birth. Mom is at risk for Pre-eclampsia and future T2DM.

steps.prompt: Maria asks about diet changes. Which instructions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Eliminate ALL carbohydrates from your diet
Eat 3 meals and 3 snacks spaced evenly
Aim for weight loss of 5-10 lbs during remainder of pregnancy
steps.rationale: We NEVER restrict calories to cause weight loss in pregnancy (ketosis hurts the baby). We spread carbs out to prevent spikes. Protein at bedtime prevents morning hypoglycemia.

steps.prompt: Post-delivery, the baby weighs 10lbs 5oz. At 1 hour of life, the baby is jittery and lethargic. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: The baby's pancreas is pumping out insulin to handle Mom's sugar. Now that the cord is cut, the sugar supply is gone, but the insulin remains. Result: Crash. Feed immediately to neutralize the insulin.

patient_profile.hpi: Timmy's mother brings him in because he has started wetting the bed again after being potty trained for years. He has lost 5 lbs in 2 weeks despite eating 'like a horse'.

ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.

patient_profile.sh: Nursing home resident. Widowed. Assistance required for ADLs.

steps.prompt: Compare the 'Honeymoon Phase' vs 'Diagnosis' in Type 1 Diabetes.
rows: Occurs shortly after starting insulin therapy
steps.rationale: After starting insulin, the rested pancreas may sputter out a last bit of insulin (Honeymoon), making control easy. Parents think 'he's cured'. He is not. It will fail completely soon.

steps.prompt: Timmy is ready for school. What must be in his backpack? Select all that apply.
options: A note excusing him from Gym class
steps.rationale: T1DM kids MUST participate in Gym (exercise helps), but they need snacks to prevent lows. Glucagon is for emergencies. Sharps are usually kept in the nurse's office, not the backpack (safety).

steps.prompt: At a birthday party, Timmy eats cake and plays tag. He gets shaky and pale. BG is 60. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Exercise increases insulin sensitivity + Cake bolus = Mismatch. Consuming simple sugar (Juice) is the Rule of 15. Glucagon is only if unconscious. Recheck in 15 mins.

patient_profile.hpi: Lt. Dan noticed a foul odor when taking off his boots. He found a rusty nail embedded in his left heel. He felt NO pain. He recalls stepping on something 'crunchy' 3 weeks ago.

ros.skin: 2cm ulcer on calcaneus. Purulent drainage. Redness ascending the ankle.
ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Retired school teacher. Lives with spouse. Nonsmoker. Occasional alcohol.

exhibits.title: Wound Culture
exhibits.content: Polymicrobial growth (Gram + Cocci and Gram - Rods).

exhibits.title: X-Ray Foot
exhibits.content: Air in soft tissue. Possible Osteomyelitis (bone infection).

steps.prompt: Differentiate Neuropathic Pain vs Ischemic Pain in the diabetic foot.
steps.rationale: Neuropathy is nerve damage (burning/numbness). Ischemia is blood flow block (pain with demand). Dan has neuropathy (didn't feel the nail).

steps.prompt: The nurse teaches foot care. Which statements by Lt. Dan indicate understanding? Select all that apply.
options: I will soak my feet in hot water daily to clean them.
I will use a mirror to inspect the bottom of my feet daily.
I will cut my toenails in a rounded curve.
I will never walk barefoot, even at home.
I will apply lotion between my toes to keep them moist.
I will shake out my shoes before putting them on.
steps.rationale: Soaking softens skin (bad). Hot water burns (he can't feel it). Cutting rounded causes ingrown nails (cut straight across). Lotion between toes causes fungus (Athlete's foot).

steps.prompt: The redness spreads up the calf. Fever 102 F. BG 350. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Ascending redness + Fever = Systemic Infection (Sepsis). Osteomyelitis is hard to cure. Antibiotics and Debridement are key. Lactate tracks sepsis severity.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: Lots of 'floaters' and sudden vision loss in right eye.
patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Wonder reports seeing 'black spiderwebs' for a week. Today, a 'curtain' fell over his right eye vision.

ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Librarian. Lives with 3 cats. Nonsmoker. Occasional glass of wine.

exhibits.title: Fundoscopy
exhibits.content: Proliferative Retinopathy. Neovascularization. Vitreous Hemorrhage visible.

steps.prompt: Match the eye complication to the symptom.
rows: Curtain falling over vision / Flashes of light
steps.rationale: High sugar damages vessels --> New weak vessels grow (Neovascularization) --> They bleed (Hemorrhage/Floaters) or pull the retina off (Detachment/Curtain).

steps.prompt: Mr. Wonder is scheduled for Vitrectomy. Which pre-op instructions are vital? Select all that apply.
options: You will need to maintain 'face-down' positioning post-op
Eye drops will be needed for weeks
steps.rationale: Bleeding is the enemy. BP and straining increase ocular pressure. Face-down positioning is for gas bubbles (often used, but straining/BP are universal priorities).

steps.prompt: Post-op, he complains of severe pain and nausea. Eye is rock hard. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Severe pain + Nausea + 'Rock Hard' Eye = Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma. The drainage is blocked. Mannitol draws fluid out. Elevation helps drain. This causes permanent blindness if not fixed.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Lee had a CT Scan with contrast 2 days ago for abdominal pain. He didn't stop his Metformin. Now he feels weak, muscles ache, and he is breathing fast.

ros.cardio: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Librarian. Lives with 3 cats. Nonsmoker. Occasional glass of wine.

steps.prompt: Analyze the drug-drug/disease interaction.
steps.rationale: Contrast dye hurts the kidneys (AKI). The kidneys excrete Metformin. If kidneys fail, Metformin builds up to toxic levels. Metformin blocks the liver from recycling lactate. Result: Lactic Acidosis.

steps.prompt: Which interventions are appropriate for Lactic Acidosis? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: Dialysis is the most effective way to remove both the Metformin and the Acid. Fluids flush the kidneys. Mucomyst protects kidneys from contrast (though late now). Insulin is for DKA, not Lactic Acidosis.

steps.prompt: Mr. Lee's pH drops to 7.10. He becomes bradycardic. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Severe acidosis stops the heart (denatures proteins/enzymes). Dialysis is the only fix. Hyperventilation (mechanical) helps blow off CO2 to compensate for the metabolic acid.
